perturbation.original_text: Charity Tunes warrants that, subject only to any limitation or condition specified in this Agreement, it will provide immediately to any consumer entitled to a music download in connection with a Pin Code the music download selected by that consumer.
perturbation.changed_text: Charity Tunes will attempt to provide a music download in connection with a Pin Code the music download selected by that consumer.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the warranty and replacing it with an attempt, the agreement weakens consumer rights regarding the provision of promised music downloads, which contradicts consumer protection laws. The original text provides a warranty, which is a form of guarantee. The modified text removes this guarantee.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Competition Act (R.S.C., 1985, c. C-34) Section 74.01(1)(a)
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 74.01(1)(a) of the Competition Act prohibits false or misleading representations to the public. By weakening the warranty, the contract creates a misleading impression about the availability of music downloads. The removal of the word "immediately" makes the contract more vague and gives Charity Tunes room to delay or deny music downloads.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Competition Act Skip to main content Skip to "About this site" Skip to section menu Marginal note: Misrepresentations to public 74.01 Â (1) Â A person engages in reviewable conduct who, for the purpose of promoting, directly or indirectly, the supply or use of a product or for the purpose of promoting, directly or indirectly, any business interest, by any means whatever, (a) Â makes a representation to the public that is false or misleading in a material respect; (b) Â makes a representation to the public in the form of a statement, warranty or guarantee of the performance, efficacy or length of life of a product that is not based on an adequate and proper test thereof, the proof of which lies on the person making the representation; (b.1) Â makes a representation to the public in the form of a statement, warranty or guarantee of a productâs benefits for protecting or restoring the environment or mitigating the environmental, social and ecological causes or effects of climate change that is not based on an adequate and proper test, the proof of which lies on the person making the representation; (b.2) Â makes a representation to the public with respect to the benefits of a business or business activity for protecting or restoring the environment or mitigating the environmental and ecological causes or effects of climate change that is not based on adequate and proper substantiation in accordance with internationally recognized methodology, the proof of which lies on the person making the representation; or (c) Â makes a representation to the public in a form that purports to be (i) Â a warranty or guarantee of a product, or (ii) Â a promise to replace, maintain or repair an article or any part thereof or to repeat or continue a service until it has achieved a specified result, if the form of purported warranty or guarantee or promise is materially misleading or if there is no reasonable prospect that it will be carried out. Marginal note: Drip pricing (1.1) Â For greater certainty, the making of a representation of a price that is not attainable due to fixed obligatory charges or fees constitut
perturbation.justification: The modification from a warranty ("Charity Tunes warrants") to an attempt ("Charity Tunes will attempt") directly contradicts consumer protection, by weakening the guarantee of providing a music download. This falls under Omission Legal Contradiction, as the contract removes the legally mandated consumer protection of a warranty. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing Section 74.01 Â (1) Â A, states that a person engages in reviewable conduct if they make a representation to the public that is false or misleading in a material respect. By weakening the guarantee, the modified text creates a misleading impression about the availability of music downloads, and thus creates a contradiction of the law. Because the original text provided a warranty, which is a form of guarantee, and the modified text removes this guarantee, this contradiction is direct and enforceable, thus the determination of YES.

perturbation.original_text: All notices required or permitted to be given under this Agreement shall be given in writing and shall be delivered, either personally or by express delivery service, to the party to be notified. Notice to each party shall be deemed to have been duly given upon delivery, personally or by courier, addressed to the attention of the officer at the address set forth heretofore, or to such other officer or addresses as either party may designate, upon at least ten days written notice, to the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: All notices may be given as deemed appropriate by either party.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the specific requirements for written notice and delivery methods, potentially violating laws requiring specific notification procedures, for contract changes or legal action notifications. Many consumer protection laws require businesses to give customers notice of certain events, such as changes to the terms of service, or data breaches. This omission introduces legal risk because it does not clearly outline how the parties must communicate changes or important information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA), S.C. 2000, c. 5, s. 10
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA requires organizations to be transparent about their personal information practices. Removing the specific notice requirements hampers transparency and makes it harder for consumers to be informed, violating PIPEDA's principle of openness. This section of PIPEDA requires that an organization make readily available to individuals specific information about its policies and practices relating to the management of personal information, including contact information. Removing detailed notice requirements hinders compliance with this aspect of the Act.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies the manner in which notices are to be given (in writing, delivered personally or by express service), ensuring a clear record and verifiable delivery. The changed text eliminates these requirements, stating that notices may be given as deemed appropriate by either party. This omission contradicts PIPEDA's principle of openness, as the law explanation suggests, because removing the specific notice requirements undermines transparency. The scraped snippet 2 generally discusses PIPEDA's requirements, including openness. While it doesn't explicitly detail notice specifics, the absence of defined procedures in the modified text makes it harder for consumers to be informed and for organizations to demonstrate compliance with PIPEDA's emphasis on readily available information regarding personal information management.

perturbation.original_text: The parties hereby attorn to the exclusive jurisdiction of the provincial and federal courts located in the city of Toronto, Ontario for all matters arising from this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties agree to resolve any disputes through binding arbitration.
perturbation.explanation: This clause removes the consumer's right to a court trial, forcing them into binding arbitration. In some jurisdictions, it must be clear that consumers are giving up rights to sue in court and agree to arbitration.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Ontario Consumer Protection Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 30, Schedule A, s. 7(1)
perturbation.law_citation: Ontario Consumer Protection Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 30, Schedule A, s. 7(1)
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 7(1) of the Ontario Consumer Protection Act outlines prohibited practices. While not directly prohibiting arbitration clauses, it generally aims to protect consumers from unfair contract terms and requires clear disclosure. Substituting court jurisdiction for mandatory binding arbitration without explicit consumer consent or clear, prominent disclosure of the implications can be seen as an unconscionable act, especially if it disadvantages the consumer.
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies court jurisdiction, while the changed text mandates binding arbitration, removing the consumer's right to a court trial. The Perturbation Explanation indicates that in some jurisdictions, it must be clear that consumers are giving up rights to sue in court and agree to arbitration. The Law Explanation supports this, suggesting that substituting court jurisdiction for mandatory binding arbitration without explicit consumer consent or clear disclosure could be seen as an unconscionable act under the Ontario Consumer Protection Act, especially if it disadvantages the consumer. This aligns with the definition of 'Omission Legal Contradiction' as the change omits a legally mandated consumer protection by removing the right to a court trial without explicit consent, making the contract non-compliant.
